data.title: Greene on Trump Iran 'civilization' post: 'Absolute madness'
data.description:
Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) on Thursday said President Trump’s threat earlier this week that Iran’s “whole civilization will die” was “absolute madness.”

CNN’s Pamela Brown and Wolf Blitzer spoke with Greene and asked why the president’s threat on Tuesday was “the final straw for you.”

“Because it’s absolute madness,” she said. “How can any person that is mentally stable call for an entire civilization of people to be murdered, to be wiped out, to never come back again? That’s what the president called for, and that shows that there’s serious instability in his thinking that he would not only say that in a private room, perhaps with his advisors, but actually go to his megaphone, his Truth Social, and post that for the entire country and the entire world?”

Trump on Tuesday warned that if Iran did not send a peace deal proposal and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a “whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again,” he wrote on Truth Social. “I don’t want that to happen, but it probably will. However, now that we have Complete and Total Regime Change, where different, smarter, and less radicalized minds prevail, maybe something revolutionarily wonderful can happen, WHO KNOWS?”

Greene said it was not Trump’s “tough rhetoric” that brought the U.S. and Iran together to agree to a shaky ceasefire deal, “it’s insanity.”

“It’s calling for the murder of an entire civilization of men, women, children, of the innocent civilians,” she continued. “That is not what I would call proper or even decent negotiations. There should be negotiations happening, and they did happen.”

The Georgia Republican noted Vice President Vance’s role in leading negotiations to lock in on the ceasefire deal with Iran. She said Vance “did not go to his social media and threaten to murder every single man, woman and child in Iran.”

“We can only presume that that would happen through nuclear bombs, because that’s the type of horrific weaponry that would be used to wipe out an entire civilization,” she said. “So no, I pushed back on that, and I think the American people fully agree. Everyone was outraged.”

Greene also slammed the ceasefire deal, saying it “doesn’t seem to be much of a ceasefire.” She said Trump, who she supported and campaigned for over the years before their feud led to her departure from Congress in January, was a “peace president” but has “gone from being the peace president to the war president, and that I don’t support at all.”

Her comments come after she told conspiracy theorist Alex Jones on Wednesday that the president “is not the same man that we supported” for 10 years.

“What has to happen is the American people have to wake up to the truth. So, they — the American people have to take the blinders off, because, look, we’ve supported Trump for 10 years now — or longer,” Greene said on Jones’ online show InfoWars.

The U.S. and Iran came to a shaky agreement for a ceasefire and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, with a few hours to spare before the deadline attached to Trump’s threat. Iran closed the strait again less than 24 hours later after Israel continued its military assault on Lebanon.

Iran’s speaker of Parliament argued that his country’s ceasefire plan applied to Lebanon too, a condition of the agreement echoed by Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif. The U.S. and Israel have denied that a pause on hostilities toward Lebanon was part of the deal.

data.title: Live updates: Trump holds troops near Iran pending 'real agreement,' rails against NATO
data.description:
President Trump said he’ll hold U.S. troops “in, and around, Iran” until the regime complies with the real agreement reached between the two countries.

“If for any reason it is not, which is highly unlikely, then the “Shootin’ Starts,” bigger, and better, and stronger than anyone has ever seen before,” Trump posted late Wednesday on Truth Social.

Meanwhile, a day after meeting with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, Trump on Thursday continued to rail against the alliance, describing it as “very disappointing” on social media.

Vice President Vance is slated to lead U.S. talks with Iran this weekend in Islamabad.

At the Pentagon, the situation is tense between Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Army Secretary Dan Driscoll after Hegseth’s purge of senior Army officials last week. Read more on the situation here.

The Rev. Al Sharpton is hosting the National Action Network (NAN) Convention, where a slew of potential 2028 candidates are speaking this week.

Trump has policy meetings and a “Make America Healthy Again” roundtable on his schedule Thursday. First lady Melania Trump is also expected to make a statement at 2:30 p.m. EDT.

The House and Senate each will convene briefly Thursday. In the House, Democrats attempted to pass their war powers resolution through unanimous consent; Republicans blocked that effort.

Follow along live below.

data.title: Lessons from COVID school aid: We need clearer goals and better accountability
data.description:
When the pandemic disrupted American K-12 schooling, Washington responded with the largest one-time federal investment in public education in American history. Three rounds of federal Elementary and Secondary School Emergency Relief funding sent $189.5 billion to K-12 schools. The goal was straightforward: help schools reopen, stabilize operations and give students a chance to recover from historic learning loss.

Now the evidence is clearer, and the conclusion is more nuanced than either side of the school-funding debate likely prefers. Pandemic aid did help — but not enough.

Research suggests that the funding produced measurable gains in achievement, especially in math. Yet those gains were modest relative to the losses students suffered. That makes the relief funds neither a clean failure nor a clear success. It was a stabilizing intervention that bought schools time and supported some recovery. It was not, by itself, a strategy capable of restoring pre-pandemic learning trajectories.

A new American Enterprise Institute working paper pushes us to the right question. The issue is not whether money matters — of course it does. The issue is whether money, sent quickly through a fragmented delivery system, can reliably produce academic recovery at scale.

The Harvard-Stanford Education Recovery Scorecard sharpens that point. Its spring 2024 analysis found a direct relationship between federal pandemic aid and district-level academic recovery in the 2022-23 school year. Each additional $1,000 in funding per student generated roughly six extra days of learning in math and three days in reading.

Related findings from the Center for Analytics of Longitudinal Data in Education Research reach similar results. There too, each additional $1,000 per student in pandemic aid was associated with roughly six extra days of learning in math, while reading gains were positive but not statistically significant.

That’s not an argument against funding schools in a crisis. It is an argument for taking implementation more seriously than federal policymakers did.

This is where the debate often goes wrong. Critics are tempted to say Washington poured nearly $190 billion into K-12 education and got little in return. Defenders are tempted to say the aid plainly helped and therefore should be judged as a success. Both claims are too blunt.

The better reading is that the funding worked the way many emergency programs work. It reduced harm, supported partial progress and exposed the weakness of the system it was trying to rescue.

Schools were not dealing only with lower test scores. They were also dealing with absenteeism, staffing disruptions, student mental health problems and frayed ties between families and institutions. Money could help districts respond to those pressures. But money alone could not solve them.

Nor should we be surprised. American education policy repeatedly assumes that resources naturally convert into outcomes. But institutions do not work that way. Funding can buy staff, tutoring contracts, curriculum materials, summer programs and social services. But funding cannot by itself create implementation quality or ensure that district leaders know which interventions to prioritize, can recruit enough people to deliver them, or can measure what is working while the crisis is still unfolding.

One of the great missed opportunities of the pandemic relief was that the country ran one of the largest education funding experiments in its history without building a strong mechanism to learn, in real time, which uses of the money produced the strongest returns. That means future emergency aid should look different the next time Washington steps in. 

Lawmakers should draw four lessons from Elementary and Secondary School Emergency Relief funds. 

First, tie emergency dollars to a recovery strategy, not just a funding stream. Federal aid should come with a recommended list of evidence-based priorities, such as tutoring, summer learning and student reengagement, rather than a broad invitation for districts to improvise.

Second, require better transparency about how the money is used. Districts should report spending in ways that allow policymakers, researchers and the public to connect categories of spending to outcomes.

Third, make attendance and student reengagement central to any recovery plan. The academic crisis after the pandemic was not only a test-score problem. It was also an absenteeism problem. Students cannot benefit from tutoring, better curriculum or extra time if they are not in school consistently.

Fourth, build state and local implementation capacity before the next crisis arrives. Money alone cannot create execution quality. States should have ready-to-launch recovery plans, approved provider lists, data systems and support structures already in place, so districts are not forced to invent solutions on the fly during an emergency.

The strongest case for the COVID education relief funding is also the strongest caution. Pandemic aid helped. But it did not come close to fully reversing the damage. If Washington sends emergency education dollars again, it should pair them with clearer goals, stronger transparency and better accountability for results.

Bruno V. Manno is a senior adviser at the Progressive Policy Institute and leads its Pathways to Opportunity What Works Lab. He is a former U.S. assistant secretary of Education for Policy.

data.title: White House confident Warsh will start as Fed chair in May
data.description:
The head of the National Economic Council (NEC) said Thursday that President Trump’s nomination of Kevin Warsh to lead the Federal Reserve is “absolutely” on track for confirmation. 

NEC Director Kevin Hassett told Fox Business Network’s Maria Bartiromo that Warsh is a “top tier candidate” for the role, but he acknowledged the current economic challenges posed by the Trump administration’s military operations against Iran. 

“I think the [interest] rates should be lower. I think my friend Kevin Warsh probably believes that still too. I haven’t talked to him in the last week or two, and that will come up in the hearing,” Hassett said, before adding that Hassett has “enormous amount of support — perhaps even bipartisan support.”

Warsh’s appointment to lead the top central bank hinges on a majority approval vote in the U.S. Senate. The Senate Banking Committee will hold a hearing April 16 to consider the nomination.

Democrats have been outspoken about their opposition to Warsh’s nomination. Additionally, GOP Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) said he will refuse to support Warsh’s nomination amid the Trump administration’s ongoing criminal investigation against current Fed Chair Jerome Powell. 

Despite this opposition, Hassett said he is “highly confident” about the future of Warsh’s nomination and said that the administration will “work out something” with Tillis. 

“I think the reason we do that is it is so important that an excellent man get there to the Fed,” the Trump official said. 

“In terms of Jay Powell, he’s really signaled that once there’s a confirmed Fed chair, that he’ll step aside, and that’s the appropriate thing for him to do,” Hassett continued, adding that he does not expect Powell to remain on the Fed’s board. 

Warsh, a former Fed board member, is an ally of the Trump administration. The president had previously said he expects the next Fed chair to consult him on interest rate decisions. The Trump administration has continued to insist on lower interest rates — pressure that Powell has resisted during his tenure. 

Hassett’s nomination comes amid global economic downturns resulting from the U.S.-Israeli joint military operations against Iran. 

Iranian counterstrikes on U.S. targets and energy infrastructure in the Gulf region have halted the flow of oil-carrying vessels through the Strait of Hormuz. The strait carries around a fifth of the world’s oil consumption every day under normal conditions, and its effective closure has spiked global energy rates. 

During his interview with Fox Business on Thursday morning, Hassett downplayed the long-term effects of these rising costs on the U.S. economy. 

“The fact is that, in terms of policy, the supply shock, like an oil shock, is widely regarded in the economic literature as to be something that you are supposed to look through, and even in the Fed minutes, it was clear that there are a lot of people who are currently at the Federal Reserve that understand that, that this is a temporary disruption and not like something that effects long run inflation rates,” the Trump official said. 

The Trump administration’s assurances have done little to quell concerns from American business leaders about the ripple effects of this conflict on the U.S. economy. 

BlackRock CEO Larry Fink warned in late March that a “global recession” could occur if the price of oil hit $150 a barrel. 

Additionally, United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby said last month that his company is preparing for oil prices exceeding $100 to continue into next year by scaling back on flights. 

“I think if you sort of start looking through what’s happening in the Middle East and how long it’s going to take to recover, even once the Straits of Hormuz are open, it seems like a reasonable assumption for us to make, and so we want to plan for something like that,” Kirby told CNBC’s Phil LeBeau. 

“We hope it’s better, but the cost of planning for something like that is pretty small,” the CEO continued. “We’re going to do a little less flying than we otherwise would, willing to leave a little bit of demand on the table if oil prices are lower, but want to be prepared for a scenario where oil prices are higher for longer.”

data.title: Centcom chief: 'Iran has suffered a generational military defeat'
data.description:
The head of U.S. Central Command (Centcom), the military arm that oversees forces in the Middle East, said Thursday that troops “remain present” in the region amid a two-week ceasefire with Iran.

Speaking in a video message posted to social media, Navy Adm. Brad Cooper said that although U.S. forces have paused offensive operations in accordance with the ceasefire, “we remain present, we remain vigilant and we remain ready if called.”

More than 50,000 American service members are in the Middle East following a nearly six-week military campaign against Iran known as Operation Epic Fury. 

President Trump late Tuesday insisted that the United States had won a “total and complete victory” against Iran after agreeing to the ceasefire deal with the country. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth claimed the same, when on Wednesday he declared “overwhelming victory” over Tehran.

But the ceasefire could give way to more fighting, Joint Chiefs of Staff Chair Gen. Dan Caine acknowledged Wednesday, noting that the U.S. military “remains ready if ordered or called upon to resume combat operations.” 

As of Thursday, the ceasefire remained in place but was tenuous due to Israel’s continued bombardment of Lebanon — which Iran said are violations of the agreement — and Tehran’s continued hold over the Strait of Hormuz.

In his message, Cooper said Iran “has suffered a generational military defeat” after the United States and Israel “systematically destroyed Iran’s ability to conduct large-scale military operations for years to come.”

“We set out to dismantle the Iranian regime’s ability to project power beyond its own borders and we clearly accomplished this task,” Cooper said, adding that Iran’s conventional military capability “has been eliminated.”

Thirteen U.S. service members died and at least 381 have been wounded since Operation Epic Fury began on Feb. 28, according to Centcom.

data.title: DNC committee shoots down resolution condemning AIPAC
data.description:
A Democratic National Committee (DNC) panel voted on Thursday to reject a resolution condemning “the growing influence” of dark money and corporate-backed outside spending in Democratic races, particularly the American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC). 

At the DNC’s spring meeting in New Orleans this week, the resolutions committee voted to kill the push, which would have been nonbinding, as scrutiny over the pro-Israel lobby grows amid the midterms. 

“The use of massive outside spending to support or oppose candidates based on their positions regarding international conflicts or foreign governments raises concerns about undue influence over democratic debate and policymaking, potentially constraining elected officials’ ability to represent the views of their constituents,” reads the resolution, submitted by Florida DNC member Allison Minnerly, pointing out AIPAC in particular for spending some $14 million in the Illinois Democratic primaries last month. 

Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker (D), who was once an AIPAC donor, condemned the group after the primaries — joining a growing number of Democrats once supportive of AIPAC who have turned on the political powerhouse over its involvement in elections this year. 

While the panel on Thursday did vote to recommend a broader resolution condemning the influence of dark money in the 2026 Democratic primary elections, it did not specifically call for AIPAC contributions to be rejected, though the attitude was largely implied. 

The resolution calls for “robust” campaign finance transparency and says that the DNC “reaffirms its commitment to campaign finance practices that align with the Party’s core values.” It further adds that the aspects of the resolution “shall inform the development of the 2028 Democratic Party Platform.”

The AIPAC resolution’s failure to advance out of the DNC committee shows that while there is an appetite within the party to take a more forceful stance against the group — an influential pro-Israel group whose opposition against conditions on aid to Israel has made the group more divisive in races more recently — it’s not one that many DNC members are comfortable standing behind. 

The DNC resolution panel’s rejection of the resolution means it will not go before the full body for a final vote on Friday. Still, AIPAC remains a wedge issue for the party.

The Israel-Hamas conflict became a focal point in the 2024 Democratic primary, as portions of the electorate voted “uncommitted” or a version of that answer against former President Biden’s initial reelection bid over anger with the administration’s policy in the region.

data.title: Lebanon to file UN complaint over Israeli strikes
data.description:
Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam said Thursday that his government will file a complaint to the United Nations Security Council concerning the latest Israeli strikes against Lebanon.

Salam said during a press conference that the Lebanese cabinet voted to submit the complaint following the strikes by the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) on Wednesday, which the Lebanese General Directorate of Civil Defense said killed at least 254 people and injured at least 1,165.

The prime minister and his cabinet also ordered the Lebanese army and security forces to strengthen protections around Beirut. 

The IDF began its latest campaign against Hezbollah on March 2, two days after the U.S. and Israel launched airstrikes against Iran. The IDF struck 100 Hezbollah targets across Lebanon on Wednesday, according to Lt. Col. Nadav Shoshani, a spokesperson. 

Heba Morayef, the regional director for the Middle East and North Africa at Amnesty International, said Wednesday marked the deadliest day in Lebanon since Israel resumed strikes last month. 

“Even before today’s attack, which the Israeli military referred to as operation ‘Eternal Darkness,’ more than 1,500 people had been killed and over a million people displaced from their homes across the country,” Morayef added.

The strikes threatened the two-week ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran, announced by President Trump on Tuesday. In response to the attacks, the Iranian government has maintained restrictions on shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, who mediated the peace talks, wrote Tuesday on social platform X that “allies” of the U.S. and Iran agreed to the ceasefire, including with respect to Lebanon. 

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian posted online Thursday, arguing that Israel’s “repeated aggression” against Lebanon is a “flagrant violation of the initial ceasefire agreement and a dangerous indicator of deceit and lack of commitment” to a wholescale end to the conflict.

“The continuation of these aggressions will render negotiations meaningless; our hands will remain on the trigger, and Iran will never abandon its Lebanese brothers and sisters,” he wrote.

The Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu wrote Tuesday on X that the ceasefire “does not include Lebanon.” Vice President Vance echoed the remarks on Wednesday, telling reporters in Hungary that the Trump administration “never indicated” that the truce applied to Israeli operations in Beruit.

“I think this comes from a legitimate misunderstanding,” he said. “I think the Iranians thought that the ceasefire included Lebanon and it just didn’t. We never made that promise.”

Vance, U.S. envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, President Trump’s son-in-law, will travel to Islamabad this weekend for talks with Iranian officials.

Netanyahu on Thursday also indicated that Israel was willing to open negotiations with the Lebanese government, according to a statement obtained by NewsNation.

data.title: Gavin Newsom's political magic: Will America fall for his tricks?
data.description:
As American servicemembers face danger in the Middle East and Iran launches missiles across the Gulf, California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) has found his moment — not to lead, mind you, but to perform.  

Within hours of the first strikes, Newsom — with whom I am well familiar, as his opponent in 2018 — was on social media condemning the war as “illegal” while simultaneously agreeing that Iran’s regime “must go.”

His team then pivoted to bragging about California gas prices. Classic Newsom: say everything, mean nothing, and make sure the cameras are rolling.

This is the Newsom I know in his natural habitat. He is, above all else, a political magician. His power lies not in accomplishing feats of genuine skill, but in distracting the audience with extraneous actions while executing sleight of hand tricks designed to generate applause.

His greatest trick yet? Vaulting his status from the incompetent and corrupt governor of a failing state to a leading contender for the 2028 Democratic presidential nomination. 

Newsom recently went on a very convenient book tour to early primary states while positioning himself as the resistance leader to Donald Trump. He is an expert at manipulating the culture wars at the same time he criticizes other politicians for their cultural positions. He’s very skilled at performing, casting himself as an everyman raised by a single mom and growing up with little in the way of creature comforts. 

This, of course, is an act totally at variance with the whole truth.  

Newsom’s father and grandfather are legendary political fixtures and fixers in California, helping to elect both Gov. Pat Brown (D) and his son, Gov. Jerry Brown (D). His grandfather obtained a lucrative concession for the Squaw Valley ski area in Lake Tahoe, which generated considerable wealth. His father was not only a close adviser to Jerry Brown but the trustee of the Getty Family Trust, which tied young Gavin to the infamous Getty family wealth. And his aunt, by marriage, is former Speaker Nancy Pelosi. 

He apparently suffers from dyslexia — a significant disability, no doubt, but not one that has held back his political ladder climbing or his accumulation of a Getty-financed chain of hotels, restaurants and wineries. Newsom’s book apparently goes into great length concerning his childhood “struggles,” and he takes great pains to highlight them for audiences so he can seem to identify with them. Classic distraction: he’s trying to get people to focus on his identity with voters instead of looking at what he has actually done when elected to our state’s highest office.

What has he done?  

Water shortages, because he’s been slow in getting reservoirs approved. Energy shortages and soaring costs, because he’s decommissioned natural gas, stopped oil drilling, and overregulated refineries while allowing huge rate increases to electric utilities.  

Housing costs have soared despite his promise to build 3.5 million new homes (the actual numbers are about 13 percent of that). Homelessness has increased despite billions wasted. Roads are some of the worst in the country despite spending far more than other states per mile. Educational results lag the worst performing states even as costs have soared.  

California has the highest taxes in the nation, with the top 1 percent paying nearly half of all income tax and set to go higher, yet still is running a massive deficit. Many are moving out. 

All of these impacts didn’t happen by accident. They are the result of the governor selling out to interest groups that benefit from these failures. Environmental lobbying groups fund Newsom’s campaigns and also promote laws like the California Environmental Quality Act that shut down energy production and water resources. Trial lawyers keep excessive litigation going, adding to the cost of living. Government employee unions want government pay and benefits to go higher and put government in control of education. All these drive up taxes and contribute to the deficit. 

Newsom will claim his state has some of the most successful businesses and wealth generation in the nation. That’s true, but that’s like the rooster claiming credit for the sunrise. California has the best weather and natural beauty in the world. That’s why smart people and businesses want to locate here, despite the incompetent and corrupt government run by special interests. 

Now, as a real crisis unfolds in the Middle East, Newsom uses it as another stage for his performance. He positions himself as both anti-war and anti-Iran, threading needles that exist only in political fantasy. He attacks Trump while offering no alternative. He claims moral authority while his own state crumbles. 

Newsom can’t escape his record with claims that he identifies with average voters, or by grandstanding on foreign policy he has no hand in shaping. Voters are looking for results. Political magic or not, his presidential ambitions deserve to go by the wayside. 

John H. Cox is an attorney, CPA, investment adviser, and former Republican nominee for governor of California. He is the author of “The Newsom Nightmare: The California Catastrophe and How to Reform Our Broken System.”

data.title: GOP blocks Democrats' attempt to limit Trump's Iran war powers
data.description:
Democrats unsuccessfully attempted to pass a measure limiting President Trump’s ability to conduct military operations in Iran during a pro forma session of the House on Thursday.

Rep. Glenn Ivey (D-Md.) sought recognition on the floor to pass the resolution from House Foreign Affairs Committee ranking member Gregoy Meeks (D-N.Y.), but Rep. Chris Smith (R-N.J.), who presided as speaker pro tempore during the session, gaveled the session out without recognizing him.

Other Democrats in attendance — Rep. Don Byer (D-Va.), Rep. Sarah Jacobs (D-Calif.), Rep. Mary Gay Scanlon (D-Pa.), Rep. Madeline Dean (D-Pa.), Rep. James Walkinshaw (D-Va.), and Rep. Suhas Subramanyam (D-Va.) — yelled in objection.

“End the war. Let us vote,” Wakinshaw said.

“The Congress needs to consider this. The time has come,” Ivey said.

Pro forma sessions of the House typically consist of a member gaveling in and out of session and last for mere minutes, conducted for reasons related to appointments and Constitutional requirements. 

While the attempt to pass the resolution by unanimous consent was expected to be unsuccessful, the move previews what will be a focus of congressional Democrats in both chambers next week as they ramp up criticism of Trump’s management of the conflict and the ceasefire deal announced Tuesday.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said Wednesday that the Senate will again vote on a resolution to limit Trump’s war powers, and the Democrats in the House are also expected to force another vote on such a measure.

The House last voted on such a measure in early March, when it defeated a resolution that would force Trump to terminate military operations against Iran until the administration could get congressional approval. Two Republicans supported it then, while four Democrats opposed it.

Since then, three of those Democrats have expressed openness to voting for the resolution, increasing the chances that it succeeds on the House floor.

data.title: DOJ investigating NFL broadcasting deals: Report
data.description:
The Department of Justice (DOJ) is opening an investigation into the NFL over its dealmaking with media companies on rights to broadcast games, according to a new report.

The Wall Street Journal, citing sources familiar with the probe, reported that the investigation from President Trump’s DOJ focuses on whether the league engaged in anticompetitive tactics that harm consumers.

Representatives for DOJ and the NFL did not immediately respond to requests for comment on Thursday’s report.

The news comes as regulators within the Trump administration, members of Congress and NFL fans across the country have raised concern about the rising cost of streaming services and cable packages required to view the league’s games.

In February, the FCC announced it was seeking public feedback on viewer experience and telegraphed an openness to changing its rules on local blackout restrictions for live sports.

That move was widely seen as a precursor to a potential rewrite of the Sports Broadcasting Act of 1961, a law granting top leagues like the NFL limited antitrust protection on certain “sponsored” telecasts when negotiating with media companies on rights to show games.

It was more expensive last season than ever to watch NFL games, with consumers paying upward of $1,000 to watch games on more than a half dozen services to see every single game.

The NFL raked in more than $100 billion with its most recent media rights deal, which is set to expire at the end of the decade. Interest in the league’s games has exploded in recent years thanks to an uptick in legal gambling services and fantasy sports.

The reported investigation comes as more major tech companies like Netflix and Amazon have poured billions into NFL broadcasts, accelerating a turn away from traditional cable bundles by fans but leading to what many consumers say is a confusing and expensive sports viewing landscape.
